prompt:
Settings store for our headless pump controller: config.c keeps key = value pairs parsed from a text blob, and the value strings live in a fixed slot pool (pool.c) because the firmware build has no allocator. cfg_load at boot has been rock solid for over a year. The live-reload path we added last sprint is another story.

Since cfg_reload got wired into the rollout tooling, settings cross-wire. Today's repro, 100% reproducible on two machines: the boot file sets timeout_ms = 2500; the rollout file changes log_level to debug, adds metrics_addr = 127.0.0.1:9100, drops cache_dir -- and does not touch timeout_ms at all. After the reload, cfg_get("timeout_ms") returns "127.0.0.1:9100". The watchdog parsed that as milliseconds and rebooted the controller.

It is not just that one key. Reloading a byte-identical file already knocks the bookkeeping loose -- pool_in_use stops agreeing with cfg_count even though nothing changed -- and once ops flip a value back and forth a few times, cfg_reload starts returning -1 outright, as if an 8-slot pool were too small for four settings. After that, reads come back wrong in ways that are bizarre but perfectly repeatable: same files, same wrong strings, every single run.

`make test` carries the exact rollout repro plus the accounting and flip-flop checks; all of it must pass. Fix the code properly -- Makefile, mintest.h and test_config.c are off-limits, and please don't paper over whatever this is by just making the pool bigger.
